text: The video displays the placement of surgical ports on the patient's abdomen, which are used for instrument access during the colectomy procedure.
The video displays three labeled working ports on the patient's abdomen, indicating their positions for the colectomy procedure.
The assistant stands on the patient's right side and utilizes the labeled port for surgical access.

The surgeon performs central ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), followed by mobilization of the splenic flexure to facilitate resection and anastomosis.
The surgeon employs a medial to lateral approach to identify and ligate the inferior mesenteric vein, located adjacent to the ligament of Treitz and just below the pancreas.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric artery and ligates it close to its origin, completing the vascular control necessary for the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs a high ligation of the central vessels, despite the absence of malignancy, to facilitate an attention-free anastomosis.
The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, carefully developing the plane between the colon mesentery and the Gerota's fascia.
The surgeon identifies and carefully preserves the ureters during the procedure, ensuring they remain intact and undisturbed.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the splenic flexure, detaching it from surrounding structures to facilitate further dissection and resection.

The pedicle ligation and splenic flexure mobilization are complete. The robotic arm in the right upper quadrant is repositioned to the left midabdomen, and the robot is redirected to the pelvis to address the colovesicle fistula.
The surgeon labels the newly established working and assistant ports on the patient's abdomen, indicating their positions for the robotic arms and surgical instruments.
The surgeon observes the rectosigmoid colon, which is densely adherent to the bladder, indicating a challenging dissection area.

The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to separate the sigmoid colon from the bladder abscess cavity and fistula tract, carefully navigating the dense adhesions between these structures.
The surgeon observes the fibrotic appearance of the abscess cavity and fistula tract, indicating chronic inflammation and tissue changes.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the sigmoid, rectosigmoid, and upper rectum, detaching these sections from surrounding tissues and structures.
The surgeon ligates and divides the mesorectum, then staples and divides an area of healthy-appearing rectum.
The surgeon resects the fibrotic fistula tract along with the thick-walled bladder wall abscess cavity, aiming to facilitate healing by removing these chronic structures.
The surgeon performs a cystotomy to expose the trigone, aiding in the resection of the bladder wall.
The surgeon closes the bladder resection and cystotomy sites using a running suture technique, applying two layers to ensure secure closure of the surgical sites.
The surgeon performs an end-to-end colorectal anastomosis, connecting the previously mobilized segments of the colon and rectum.
The surgeon places a surgical drain in the pelvic cavity, ensuring proper positioning for postoperative fluid drainage.
